title: A Big Shift in the AI Race
dek: Anthropic's Mythos/Fable shutdown drags into its fifth day with no resolution in sight — while Elon Musk uses SpaceX's blockbuster IPO to swallow Cursor for $60B and reshape the model race. The throughline: AI is being pulled ever deeper under national security control, and the race just got more dynamic than anyone expected.

oneIdea.headline: The AI race just got dynamic again — and SpaceX is the one rewriting it.
oneIdea.summary: Just as everyone settled into the agentic phase, the board reshuffled. Anthropic is stuck in a regulatory standoff, OpenAI's leaked financials reveal a profitable inference business hiding under accounting noise, and Elon Musk parlayed a 49%-pop SpaceX IPO into a $60B Cursor acquisition. The connective tissue: AI infrastructure, frontier models, and access are increasingly being placed under national security control — and the race is far more fluid than the 'settled' sentiment suggested.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: The model under your scribe could be switched off by a government, not a vendor
items.body: The hardest fact in today's edition is not financial — it is that a frontier model was pulled worldwide for foreign nationals by government order, the first kill switch of its kind. Most AI scribes sit on top of one of these big models. For a UK practice that is a continuity question worth asking your supplier plainly: which underlying model do you depend on, and what is your fallback if it is suddenly restricted? Keep a manual note-taking habit alive so a clinic is never left without documentation if access changes overnight.

items.title: If Zitron is right, expect price rises; if NLW is right, expect more model churn
items.body: If Zitron's reading holds and OpenAI's losses really are structural, the bill eventually lands on customers — so a scribe contract renewal may bring higher prices or tighter usage caps; budget defensively. If NLW's reading holds and the inference business is genuinely profitable, the bigger disruption is competitive churn — cheaper rivals like the Cursor-style entrants undercutting incumbents — which means the scribe that is best value today may not be in a year. Either way, avoid long lock-ins and keep your data portable.

items.title: 'Profitable on inference' is the number to watch, and it has a clinical analogue
items.body: NLW's overlooked point — that selling tokens may already be profitable even while the company loses money overall — is the right lens for judging any AI tool's durability: separate the running cost of the service you actually use from the vendor's investment burn. A scribe vendor can be sustainable on the per-consultation cost even if its parent is haemorrhaging cash on training. When assessing tools for the practice, ask for the unit economics of the service you consume, not the headline valuation.

the_other_side.source: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
the_other_side.text: This is the very leak NLW builds half his episode on — Zitron is the one who obtained and published OpenAI's audited 2025 figures, verified by the Financial Times. He reads them as terminal where NLW reads them as misunderstood. By his account OpenAI took $13.07 billion in revenue against $34 billion in total costs and a $20.92 billion operating loss, with research and development alone at $19.18 billion. He stresses that operating losses are 'mounting year-over-year at a dramatic rate' and says plainly he is 'not sure how this company finds a way toward any kind of sustainability or profitability.' Where NLW lifts out the inference line — $13 billion of revenue on $7.5 billion cost of revenue — as the overlooked good-news story, Zitron treats it as a rounding detail beside a loss from operations that dwarfs every dollar of revenue.

the_truth_beneath.text: Both men orbit one variable: whether OpenAI's headline loss is a fundamentally unprofitable business or accounting noise sitting on top of a viable core. They accept the same audited facts — $13.07bn revenue, $7.5bn cost of revenue, $34bn total costs, a $20.92bn operating loss, and a one-time $41.55bn fair-value charge from the for-profit conversion that inflates the $38.5bn headline. The empirical question that resolves it is whether, once that one-off charge is stripped out, the operating loss narrows as inference gross profit scales faster than R&D and sales grow; the observable is OpenAI's next audited statement or IPO prospectus showing the ex-one-off operating-margin trajectory. The residue is pure worldview: is the gap between cost and value structural, or a phase of investment ahead of a real engine? NLW is vindicated if the inference profit scales and operating losses shrink ex the conversion charge; Zitron if losses keep mounting with no margin path. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW anti-doom and reaching for the bullish read, Zitron bubble-certain and the man who chose to publish the leak — yet both accept the same numbers and even agree the $13bn-on-$7.5bn inference line is real; they differ only on whether it matters. Watch the next audited disclosure and whether the operating loss, stripped of the conversion charge, actually falls.

headlines.headline: Anthropic sends hackers, not lobbyists, to Washington
headlines.summary: Five days into the Mythos/Fable shutdown, Anthropic's leaders touched down in DC for talks that appear largely technical — sending Chief Compute Officer Tom Brown, External Affairs head Sarah Heck, red-teaming head Logan Graham, and security researcher Nicholas Carlini. The makeup of the delegation hints at how Anthropic is framing the dispute.

headlines.headline: It's pretty clear to me that these current models are better vulnerability researchers than I am.
headlines.attribution: Nicholas Carlini, Anthropic security researcher (via The Wall Street Journal)
headlines.summary: The WSJ profiled Nicholas Carlini, a respected hacker and professional AI-skeptic who changed his mind after using Mythos to find never-before-discovered bugs in Ghost and Linux. He warned the attacker-defender balance of the last two decades 'seems like it's probably coming to an end.'

headlines.headline: This is a Mythos ban, not just a Fable ban
headlines.summary: Commerce signaled willingness to let consumer-facing Fable come back online once Anthropic fixes the jailbreak — but Mythos is another question entirely. The re-release of Fable is actually fairly low on the government's list of concerns.

headlines.headline: A South Korean telecom may have triggered the schism
headlines.summary: The Washington Post reports Anthropic added ~50 firms to Project Glasswing weeks ago, then took days to identify the new recipients. When the government got the list, it found a South Korean telecom suspected of ties to the Chinese government — prompting officials to consider export controls to claw back the technology.

headlines.headline: They came to every fork in the road and took the wrong fork.
headlines.summary: An administration official told Axios that Anthropic knew the model was susceptible to a jailbreak and distributed it anyway. Another source was blunter: 'Everybody said Anthropic was a bad actor... Now those people are questioning that. They screwed us.'

headlines.headline: "This means we can't have the model out." "That's the point."
headlines.attribution: Dario Amodei and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick (via Bloomberg)
headlines.summary: Bloomberg published the full Commerce Department letter, which ordered Anthropic to remove access for all foreign nationals wherever located under threat of criminal and civil penalties. When Dario Amodei reportedly told Secretary Lutnick the order meant pulling the model, Lutnick's response was 'That's the point.'

headlines.headline: This ad hoc last-minute licensing regime is bad for companies, the administration, and the public.
headlines.attribution: Charlie Bullock, Institute for Law and AI
headlines.summary: Charlie Bullock of the Institute for Law and AI called the legal theory 'strange, very aggressive, and probably vulnerable to legal and constitutional challenge' — but predicted Anthropic won't sue and the two sides will eventually resolve it. The deeper problem: a regulatory regime that isn't really a regulatory regime, and the urgent need for actual legislation.

headlines.headline: Anthropic is negotiating with a regulator without realizing it.
headlines.summary: Ashley (@Polilythia) argued Anthropic scoped its risk too broadly, and when its mitigation proved insufficient for that stated scope, tried to narrow it — a huge red flag to any regulator. So regulatory action followed.

headlines.headline: Managing Washington is now Anthropic's job — and they may not realize it
headlines.summary: NLW's read: you can be mad at who's in power and at the government's lack of technical capacity, but a company seen as integral to the economy and national security doesn't get to fail at dealing with the government in power. The charitable interpretation is that Anthropic simply hasn't recognized that managing this relationship is now as important as anything they build in the lab.

headlines.headline: "What the hell were you doing for the other hour and 15 minutes?"
headlines.summary: NLW points to a pattern of slow Anthropic responses — days to deliver the Glasswing recipient list, and even the friendly counter-narrative that Dario took 'only' an hour and 15 minutes to call back on Friday night. When the government calls about something this mission-critical, the move is to pause everything and get on the phone.

headlines.headline: Washington has just done more to boost Chinese AI models than Beijing ever could have hoped.
headlines.attribution: Agatha Demere, Council on Foreign Relations (in the Financial Times)
headlines.summary: Cybersecurity experts (100+ signatures on an open letter) say removing Mythos from the cyber-defense toolkit makes everyone more vulnerable, and geopolitical analysts call it a gift to China. Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid warned: 'You can't rely on something that could be switched off.'

headlines.headline: You cannot fully fix jailbreaks in these models.
headlines.attribution: Helen Toner, former OpenAI board member
headlines.summary: The administration is sticking to its demand for a jailbreak patch — but it's not clear that's even technically possible. The FT also reports the ban has thrown the US research community into a tailspin, since it bars foreign-national researchers from working on the most advanced models.

headlines.headline: The frontier CEOs are all at a G7 lunch this week
headlines.summary: Politico reports a two-and-a-half-hour G7 lunch in France with CEOs including Dario Amodei, Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, and Mistral's Arthur Mensch. The official agenda is economic growth and resilient societies — but it's hard to believe the real conversation won't be about Washington.

headlines.headline: We're in a very liminal moment for AI
headlines.summary: NLW frames a broad realignment: companies grappling with what full agentic workloads will cost and racing to become token-efficient, a US economy whose GDP growth leans heavily on AI infrastructure build-out, and the political dimension all converging at once. The economics of mature agentic AI are starting to become clear.

headlines.headline: Neo-cloud revenue became SpaceX's #1 source overnight
headlines.summary: After folding xAI into SpaceX looked to many like a bailout, the calculus flipped: building Colossus II created an asset SpaceX could monetize easily. Compute deals with Anthropic and then Google made neo-cloud revenue its top line basically overnight, and the orbital-data-center narrative suddenly made the IPO make sense.

headlines.headline: SpaceX pops 49%, becomes 5th-largest company on Earth
headlines.summary: SpaceX closed Tuesday at $201.80, up 49% from its IPO price, valuing the company at $2.6 trillion — just ahead of Amazon. Critics note Amazon has roughly 40x the revenue, and that the vast majority of SpaceX stock is still locked, which could create bearish pressure as it unlocks through the year.

headlines.headline: With today's 20% SpaceX pop, Elon made more money today than Warren Buffett made in his entire career.
headlines.attribution: Ryan Petersen, Flexport CEO, on X
headlines.summary: On the back of the IPO, Musk became the world's first trillionaire, roughly 3x ahead of the next-richest person, though most of his wealth is locked in a 46% SpaceX stake he couldn't liquidate without tanking the price. Flexport's Ryan Petersen later clarified his post came before the stock rose another 14% after hours.

headlines.headline: Value begets value, talent begets talent.
headlines.summary: Bill Ackman argued that one of the things that makes SpaceX so valuable is how valuable it is — the Cursor acquisition cost materially less in dilution thanks to SpaceX's high valuation, enabling accretive deals and attracting top talent.

headlines.headline: SpaceX buys Cursor for $60B
headlines.summary: SpaceX exercised its right to acquire Cursor, which becomes a wholly owned subsidiary at a $60B price. Cursor had hit a $4B run rate growing 7x year over year — and the FT suggests the deal could be SpaceX's 'Instagram moment,' taking a hot competitor off the market early.

headlines.headline: Cursor teases a from-scratch model, no more Kimi base
headlines.attribution: Nick Dobos, engineer, at the Compile event
headlines.summary: Cursor moved from harness-only to playing the model game with its Composer line (matching Opus 4.7 / GPT-5.5 at ~a tenth of the cost via post-training on a Kimi K base). Now it's teasing a model trained from scratch — same size as Claude Opus 5.5, 10-20x more compute than Composer, 'generally intelligent, not just coding,' releasing in weeks.

headlines.headline: The focus over the next few years will be firmly on the control plane.
headlines.summary: Chamath Palihapitiya argued that despite Cursor's model push, it's the harness and customer relationship that matter — giving organizations the governance, control, auditability, and business continuity to go all in on AI. He called the SpaceX-Cursor merger the first big exit at the application layer, 'but not the last.'

headlines.headline: DOJ defends xAI's gas turbines as too vital to shut off
headlines.summary: The government intervened in the NAACP's Clean Air Act suit against xAI's Colossus II turbines, with the DOJ arguing a shutdown 'threatens American national, economic, and energy security.' A Pentagon official said Grok supported 'vital national security missions,' including targeting decisions for recent strikes on Iran — and Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI models are also cleared for classified use.

headlines.headline: Two June events look unrelated but are actually the same story.
headlines.summary: Chubby on X tied the Mythos/Fable foreign-national shutdown and the DOJ's defense of unpermitted gas turbines into one thread: AI and everything around it — data centers, frontier models, access — is increasingly being placed under national security and control.

headlines.headline: OpenAI's leaked numbers: $38.5B loss, mostly accounting
headlines.summary: Ed Zitron published OpenAI's audited figures: ~$13B revenue in 2025 against ~$21B operational losses and a $38.5B net loss. But OpenAI says the majority came from a one-time ~$30B non-cash accounting charge tied to its conversion to a public benefit corporation — strip that and stock comp out, and the loss was about $8B.

headlines.headline: The overlooked story: OpenAI is profitable on inference
headlines.summary: The number both the skeptic and the FT missed: OpenAI appears to turn a tidy profit selling tokens. In 2025 it generated $13B in revenue on $7.5B of direct cost of revenue (2024: $3.7B on $2.7B). Too simplistic to ignore training and staffing, but a promising sign of solid margins in the core business.

headlines.headline: OpenAI's cash pile could let it delay its IPO
headlines.summary: The Information reports OpenAI spent $3.7B in Q1 (excluding $8.6B in training costs), holding burn roughly steady. With $73B in cash and marketable securities — up from $40B in December — and Sam Altman insisting no IPO timeline is set, it's easy to imagine OpenAI staying private longer amid SpaceX unlocks and government friction.

headlines.headline: The 'settled' agentic phase is anything but
headlines.summary: NLW's wrap: just as it felt like the agentic phase had settled in, the race is clearly fast-moving. Things to watch — resolution of Anthropic's Fable issue, whether OpenAI avoids its own version of it, what SpaceX's public price does, what Cursor's next model reveals about strategy, and a conspicuously quiet Google that won't stay quiet for long.
